zones.name: Branch Collection Pipeline
zones.description: Orchestrates branch-work data collection and exposes it through the PR-markdown CLI command.
insights: Low cohesion (0.2) — files are loosely related, consider splitting this zone
High coupling (0.8) — 4 imports target "sourcevision-analysis-core"
The pairing of branch-work-collector.ts (a service) with pr-markdown.ts (a CLI command) in one zone reveals a thin pipeline: collect → render — making the data flow easy to trace

Low cohesion (0.20) and high coupling (0.80) signal that this zone functions as a thin adapter/orchestration layer rather than an encapsulated module, which is architecturally valid but should be explicit
Four imports flowing into unit-2 and three into unit suggest this zone is downstream of both core zones — confirm it never imports back into them to keep the DAG acyclic

Cohesion 0.20 and coupling 0.80 are at warning thresholds; as an orchestration-layer adapter this is acceptable if the zone deliberately wires collector → CLI, but document this intent to prevent accidental logic accumulation.

The CLI command (pr-markdown.ts) and its domain service (branch-work-collector.ts) are correctly co-located here, keeping the CLI thin and the domain logic in unit; this is a positive structural pattern.
Zone "branch-collection-pipeline" has files across 5 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory

Only appears in cross-zone imports as 'unit-analyzers → unit-2' and 'unit-analyzers → unit', suggesting branch-collection-pipeline's outbound coupling flows entirely through unit-2 as an intermediary — it never directly touches unit or unit-cli, making it an indirect downstream consumer

branch-collection-pipeline connects to the rest of the codebase exclusively via unit-2 as a single coupling point — this makes unit-2 a mandatory transitive dependency for any consumer of the collection pipeline, increasing blast radius if unit-2 is refactored.

src/cli/commands/pr-markdown.ts (CLI layer) and branch-work-collector.ts (domain service) occupy the same zone — CLI commands are leaf consumers and domain services are reusable logic; their co-location prevents the domain service from being used without also loading CLI machinery

tests/integration/pr-markdown-reviewer-output.test.ts tests markdown rendering output, which is a presentation concern owned by pr-markdown-template.ts in branch-risk-domain — the test is misassigned to the collection pipeline zone rather than to the template zone it actually exercises

CLI command (pr-markdown.ts) and domain service (branch-work-collector.ts) are in the same zone — split along the CLI/domain boundary so the domain service can be imported independently of CLI infrastructure

tests/integration/pr-markdown-reviewer-output.test.ts validates markdown rendering output but is assigned to the collection pipeline zone — misassigned test obscures which production code it guards and should be co-located with the template code in branch-risk-domain

Every file in this zone lives in a different directory: src/analyzers/, src/cli/commands/, tests/integration/, tests/unit/analyzers/, tests/unit/cli/ — the zone has zero directory co-location (5 files, 5 directories), meaning import-graph proximity is the sole binding force; directory-level navigation provides no indication that these files form a coherent unit

5 files across 5 distinct directories with zero directory co-location — the zone is held together only by import proximity; evaluate dissolving it entirely by pinning branch-work-collector.ts to the domain zone it couples with and pr-markdown.ts to a cli-commands zone, rather than treating the glue layer as an architectural unit
[call graph] 213 internal calls, 5 outgoing, 2 incoming (cohesion: 0.98, coupling: 0.02)

zones.name: Branch Risk Domain
zones.description: Core domain layer encompassing branch-work classification, filtering, storage, risk scoring, PR markdown templating, and the package's public API surface.
insights: High coupling (0.52) — 8 imports target "sourcevision-analysis-core"
This zone owns the public.ts entrypoint and schema/v1.ts, making it the canonical API surface — its health is critical to downstream consumers

Medium cohesion (0.48) with medium coupling (0.52) is acceptable for a domain hub, but the combination of store, classifier, filter, risk-scorer, and template in one zone suggests it could be split into a pure-domain core and a presentation/generation sub-layer
The bidirectional 8-import exchange with unit-2 is a red flag — domain hubs should export to consumers, not mutually depend on them
Bidirectional coupling with unit-2 (8 imports in each direction) creates a circular dependency smell at the zone level; decompose shared types into a neutral foundation zone to break the cycle.

Hosting both the public API entrypoint (public.ts) and implementation details (branch-work-store, risk-scoring) in the same zone makes the public surface hard to audit; consider pinning entrypoint and schema files to a dedicated api-surface zone.
Zone "branch-risk-domain" has files across 7 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory

Hosts public.ts (the package's canonical API entrypoint) while being embedded in a bidirectional cycle with unit-2 — the public API surface is directly entangled in cyclic coupling, making the exported interface unstable and hard to audit

public.ts residing in a zone that participates in an 8+8 bidirectional cycle with unit-2 means the package's public API is coupled to internal implementation cycles — pin public.ts and schema/v1.ts to an isolated api-surface zone with only downward imports to stabilize the contract boundary.

pr-markdown-template.ts is a markdown rendering artifact (presentation layer) co-located with domain classifiers, a risk scorer, and a store — presentation templates have no business in a domain zone and create an implicit dependency from domain on formatting concerns

branch-work-store.ts introduces stateful, mutable persistence into the same zone as stateless classifiers and filters — stores and pure-function domain logic operate at different abstraction levels and should be separated to maintain referential transparency in the domain layer

schema/v1.ts is the package's canonical data contract but lives inside a domain-implementation zone that participates in bidirectional cycles — the schema is therefore reachable only through a zone that carries implementation baggage, making it impossible to depend on the contract without also depending on domain logic

pr-markdown-template.ts (presentation/rendering) is embedded in the domain zone alongside classifiers and risk-scorers — presentation templates should reside in a dedicated output or generators zone, not in the domain layer

branch-work-store.ts co-locates stateful persistence with stateless domain logic (classifier, filter, risk-scorer) — the store abstraction belongs in a separate persistence or state zone to preserve the functional purity of the domain layer

schema/v1.ts (the package's canonical data contract) lives inside a zone that participates in an 8+8 bidirectional cycle with unit-2 — the schema is transitively entangled in implementation cycles, making it impossible to depend on the contract without pulling in all of branch-risk-domain's coupling baggage; extract schema/v1.ts to an isolated schema-foundation zone with zero outbound runtime dependencies

public.ts zone membership is determined by Louvain community detection on the import graph — a non-deterministic algorithm assigns the package's most critical file, meaning a graph topology change (adding or removing a single import) could silently reassign public.ts to a different zone in a future analysis run, changing which zone 'owns' the public API without any visible code change

public.ts entrypoint zone assignment is algorithm-determined and not stable across re-analysis runs — pin public.ts to a manually-curated zone in the zone manifest to prevent silent entrypoint drift after future graph topology changes
[call graph] 819 internal calls, 132 outgoing, 3 incoming (cohesion: 0.86, coupling: 0.14)

zones.name: Claude Workspace Config
zones.description: Claude Code IDE permission and local settings configuration for the workspace.
insights: Isolated files — no import edges between 2 files, cohesion is unmeasurable (reported as 0)
Contains only two JSON settings files with no imports, forming an isolated configuration island
Split between settings.json (checked in) and settings.local.json (likely gitignored) follows good secret hygiene
No production code — treat as dev tooling metadata, not an architectural zone in the traditional sense
Zero cohesion and zero coupling are expected here; this zone is configuration-only and should not be evaluated against code quality thresholds.
Forms a perfect zero-coupling island with workspace-root — together these two zones define a clean 'no-import' boundary layer that all other zones correctly ignore

claude-workspace-config and workspace-root together form a cleanly isolated configuration stratum with zero cross-zone imports in either direction — this boundary is the healthiest in the codebase and should be explicitly preserved.

zones.name: Data File Schema
zones.description: A minimal schema module defining canonical data-file constants (DATA_FILES, SUPPLEMENTARY_FILES) with co-located unit tests for MCP and schema validation.
insights: High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
At 3 files with perfect cohesion and zero coupling, this micro-zone is well-isolated but small enough to question whether it warrants its own zone rather than being absorbed into the analysis core.
The single production file (src/schema/data-files.ts) is imported 7 times from Sourcevision Analysis Core, confirming it serves as a shared constant registry rather than a domain module.

Co-locating two test files (mcp.test.ts, data-files.test.ts) with the production schema file is what keeps this zone's cohesion at 1.0 — if tests were separated, this would collapse into the core zone.
Perfect cohesion and zero coupling make this an ideal candidate to pin as a foundation-level constant module, explicitly positioned below the analysis core in the dependency hierarchy.

The mcp.test.ts file tests CLI MCP behavior but lives in this schema zone due to import-graph proximity — verify it belongs here architecturally or consider moving it closer to the CLI entry point tests.
Seven inbound imports from the analysis core confirm data-files.ts is a shared constant dependency; keeping it isolated here prevents the constants from being entangled with analyzer logic.

The zero coupling score despite 7 inbound imports confirms this zone is a true leaf: it exports constants but depends on nothing — a rare and stable pattern that should be preserved as the package grows

The co-location of mcp.test.ts in this schema zone introduces an implicit maintenance coupling: changes to CLI MCP behavior require touching a file that lives outside the CLI zone, making the test harder to discover during CLI refactors

data-file-schema is the only non-fixture zone with zero outbound cross-zone imports — it is the sole genuine foundation layer in this codebase and should be explicitly protected from gaining any outbound edges
mcp.test.ts lives in data-file-schema but tests CLI MCP behavior — its zone placement is an artifact of import-graph proximity, not architectural intent, and will silently diverge as CLI code evolves

The zone name 'data-file-schema' describes implementation structure (data files) rather than functional purpose (shared constant registry) — renaming to 'constants-registry' or 'shared-constants' would clarify why other zones import from it

If mcp.test.ts were relocated to the CLI test zone, data-file-schema would shrink to 2 files (one production, one test), making it one of the smallest possible zone configurations — at that size, it could be absorbed into the analysis core without losing the isolation benefit that currently justifies its existence

mcp.test.ts is discoverable only by import-graph proximity, not by file path or zone membership. Developers debugging CLI MCP behavior will search the CLI zone for tests and miss this file entirely, leading to duplicate test authoring or untested regression paths during CLI refactors.

Zone cohesion of 1.0 is structurally artificial: it is maintained by co-locating test files (mcp.test.ts, data-files.test.ts) with the single production source file rather than by genuine functional import density. If tests were relocated to the tests/ directory consistent with every other zone, cohesion would drop and this micro-zone would likely dissolve into the core during the next Louvain pass.

data-files.ts has zero outbound imports (correct for a constant registry) but the zone-level isolation this creates is fragile: any developer adding a single import to data-files.ts would silently destroy the only genuine foundation-layer zone in the package with no automated detection

Zone cohesion 1.0 is an artifact of test file co-location with production source, not genuine functional cohesion. This creates an inconsistency: every other zone stores tests under tests/, but data-file-schema stores them alongside src/. Documenting this as an intentional pattern (or relocating the tests) prevents future maintainers from normalizing the co-location and accidentally dissolving the zone boundary.

No automated assertion prevents data-files.ts from gaining outbound imports. A single accidental import in this file would destroy the only zero-coupling foundation zone in the package. Adding a test that asserts data-files.ts has no non-type imports would protect the invariant that makes this zone valuable.
[call graph] 5 internal calls, 7 outgoing, 0 incoming (cohesion: 0.42, coupling: 0.58)

zones.name: Sourcevision Analysis Core
zones.description: The primary sourcevision package implementation spanning the full analysis pipeline, schema types, CLI interface, and report export.
insights: High cohesion (0.9) — files are tightly interconnected
Contains 60% of project files (87/146) — subdivided into 7 sub-zones
11 entry points — wide API surface, consider consolidating exports
With 87 files and cohesion of 0.9, this zone is the dominant core of the sourcevision package — consolidating analyzers, schema, CLI commands, and PDF export into a tightly coupled unit.

The 12 outbound imports to 'Static Analysis Services' (analyzers) combined with 5 inbound returns suggest those 8 files may belong here rather than in a separate zone — Louvain likely over-partitioned.

The bidirectional import relationship with 'Branch Risk Domain' (unit, 8 each way) is the key coupling concern to resolve — it indicates a shared-state or utility dependency that should be extracted into a lower-level module.
Zone has exemplary internal cohesion (0.9) and very low external coupling (0.1), indicating a well-bounded implementation layer.

Bidirectional imports with both 'Static Analysis Services' (12 out / 5 in) and 'Branch Risk Domain' (8 each way) create zone-level cycles that obscure actual dependency direction — the shared dependencies should be pushed into a common foundation module.

Entry points span schema, CLI, and analyzer layers simultaneously, which is appropriate for a package core but means consumers must navigate multiple entry surfaces — the public.ts barrel should remain the canonical single entry point.
Zone "sourcevision-analysis-core" has files across 12 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory

unit-cli creates a three-node cycle with unit-2: unit-2 exports 7 imports to unit-cli, and unit-cli re-imports back to unit-2 (1) and unit (1), closing a cycle through a third party — the most structurally fragile pattern in this batch

All five named zones (unit, unit-2/core, unit-cli, analyzers, unit-analyzers) route traffic through sourcevision-analysis-core, making it a mandatory intermediary — any breaking change here has blast radius across the entire package graph

unit-cli participates in a three-node cycle: unit-2 → unit-cli (7 imports) and unit-cli → unit-2 (1 import) closes a direct cycle, while unit-cli → unit (1 import) and unit → unit-2 (8 imports) closes a second indirect cycle through unit — two overlapping cycles sharing one edge

sourcevision-analysis-core is the sole mandatory intermediary for all cross-zone traffic in the package — zero zones communicate with each other without passing through it, making it a single point of architectural failure for any zone-boundary refactor

At 87 files and cohesion 0.9, this zone functionally IS the package — zone detection has produced a near-trivial partition where one zone contains 60% of all files with near-perfect internal cohesion, rendering the zone boundary semantically meaningless for architectural analysis

The zone name 'sourcevision-analysis-core' is tautological (core of the sourcevision analysis package) — it describes the container, not a functional domain, making zone-level discussions indistinguishable from package-level discussions

The 7 sub-zones mentioned as internal subdivisions are doing more architectural work than the top-level zone boundary itself — they should be promoted to top-level zones to make the partition actionable

Zone encompasses 87 files at cohesion 0.9, effectively absorbing the entire package into a single unit. Zone detection has failed to partition the codebase into actionable boundaries — the 7 internal sub-zones should be elevated to top-level zones so that cross-zone import analysis reflects real architectural intent rather than a single monolithic blob vs. a few satellite micro-zones.

The 'analyzers' sub-zone (8 files, cohesion 0.21) has the lowest cohesion of any named zone, indicating its 8 files do not form a natural cluster. Files grouped there likely share a directory path rather than import relationships — cohesion below 0.3 in an 8-file zone suggests artificial grouping that obscures actual dependencies.

unit-2 imports from both data-file-schema (foundation layer, correct) AND unit-cli (leaf/CLI layer, incorrect) — a barbell import profile that makes this zone structurally ambiguous: it simultaneously behaves as a domain layer consumer and a CLI layer consumer, which prevents assigning it a clear tier position in any enforced layering contract

The zone absorbs 76% of all cross-zone traffic yet has no dedicated public API barrel or index.ts that would make its external surface explicit — consumers import arbitrary internal files, meaning the zone boundary exists only in community-detection metadata, not in code structure

At 87 files and cohesion 0.9, this zone is internally well-structured but externally ungoverned — the high internal cohesion actively masks the severity of its external coupling problems by making the interior feel clean while the boundary remains porous

Zone imports from unit-cli (7 imports: domain consuming leaf/CLI layer) while also correctly importing from data-file-schema (foundation). This barbell profile — consuming both the foundation layer and the leaf layer simultaneously — means the zone has no stable tier position and cannot be isolated for independent versioning or replacement.

No index.ts or public barrel file exists at the zone boundary to make the external API surface explicit. All 87 files are directly importable by other zones, meaning zone membership is enforced only by community-detection metadata, not by code structure. Any new import added to any of the 87 files silently extends the zone boundary.

zones.name: Static Analysis Services
zones.description: Core analysis infrastructure providing archetype detection, call-graph finding generation, completion reading, and manifest management.
insights: Low cohesion (0.21) — files are loosely related, consider splitting this zone
High coupling (0.79) — 5 imports target "sourcevision-analysis-core"

The mix of constants.ts (types) with four distinct service files (archetypes, callgraph-findings, completion-reader, manifest) suggests these services share foundational type contracts but have divergent responsibilities
High coupling (0.79) combined with low cohesion (0.21) indicates this zone acts as a cross-cutting utility layer imported widely across the codebase rather than a tightly scoped module
Test files for three of the four services are co-located, which is a positive sign — consider adding coverage for callgraph-findings.ts which appears untested

Cohesion 0.21 and coupling 0.79 exceed warning thresholds; the zone is pulled in by 12 imports from unit-2 alone, suggesting it may be serving as a dumping ground for shared analysis primitives rather than a focused module.
callgraph-findings.ts appears to have no corresponding test file while its sibling services (archetypes, completion-reader, manifest) are all tested — close this coverage gap.

Receives 12 imports from unit-2 while sending 5 back — this asymmetric bidirectional flow means unit-2 is the dominant consumer but analyzers is not purely subordinate, creating a soft circular dependency at zone level

The 12-inbound / 5-outbound import asymmetry with unit-2 indicates analyzers acts as a shared utility layer that has grown a back-dependency on its primary consumer — extract the shared contracts into a neutral schema zone to make the relationship strictly one-directional.

constants.ts acts as a shared type registry but is embedded inside the service zone — any zone needing only the type contracts must take a dependency on the entire static-analysis-services zone, pulling in unneeded service code as a side effect

completion-reader.ts name implies disk or stream I/O (reading completion artifacts) rather than pure analysis transformation — mixing I/O behavior with stateless analysis services prevents hermetic unit testing of either concern

constants.ts defines shared type contracts but is co-located with service implementations, forcing consumers to couple to the full service zone to access shared types — extract to a neutral schema or types zone with zero runtime dependencies

completion-reader.ts introduces file-system I/O into a zone labeled as analysis services — I/O adapters and pure analysis logic are different abstraction levels and should reside in separate zones to allow hermetic testing

src/constants.ts resides in the src/ root directory while every other file in this zone lives in src/analyzers/ — the zone silently spans two directory levels, and constants.ts is the sole src/-root occupant, making its zone membership invisible to directory-based navigation

The zone simultaneously acts as a type registry (constants.ts), an I/O adapter (completion-reader.ts), and a set of stateless analysis transformers (archetypes, callgraph-findings, manifest) — three distinct abstraction layers coexisting in one zone; this structural triality mechanically explains why cohesion lands at 0.21 despite all files being imported together

src/constants.ts sits in src/ root while all peer files live in src/analyzers/ — relocate to src/analyzers/constants.ts or a dedicated src/schema/ directory to make zone membership visible through directory structure alone without requiring tooling

Three distinct abstraction layers (type registry via constants.ts, I/O adapter via completion-reader.ts, stateless transformers via archetypes/manifest/callgraph-findings) coexist in one zone and mechanically produce cohesion 0.21 — dissolve the zone by assigning each abstraction tier to its appropriate target zone rather than attempting an internal refactor

zones.name: Test Fixture Projects
zones.description: Standalone sample projects (a Remix app and a small TypeScript project) used as realistic inputs for sourcevision integration and end-to-end tests.
insights: High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
Complete isolation (cohesion: 1.0, coupling: 0, no cross-zone imports) is exactly correct for fixture data — it should never import from production source.
Two fixture projects cover distinct surface areas: a Remix route-based app tests React component discovery, while the small-ts-project tests basic module and service graph analysis.

As sourcevision analysis capabilities grow, the fixture set should expand in parallel — each new analyzer feature (callgraph, PDF export) should have a representative fixture project to exercise it in e2e tests.
Fixtures are fully self-contained with no production imports, which is the correct pattern — any drift toward importing from src/ would indicate test infrastructure coupling and should be reverted.
The Remix fixture provides a realistic multi-file route hierarchy for testing React component and import-graph analyzers against non-trivial real-world structure.
There is no fixture for a project with circular dependencies or monorepo structure — adding one would improve confidence in the zone-detection and cycle-detection paths.
Zone "test-fixture-projects" has files across 5 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory

No fixture exercises the zone-detection algorithm against a project with pre-existing circular imports — the one scenario most likely to produce incorrect zone boundaries in production is unrepresented in e2e coverage
No fixture models a nested-package or monorepo layout, leaving the inventory phase's package.json boundary detection untested at the e2e level

test-fixture-projects is the only zone with confirmed zero cross-zone coupling in both directions — it is the reference model for what a correctly bounded zone looks like and its pattern (path-prefixed isolation, no src/ imports) should be codified as a lint rule

Fixture coverage gap: no fixture project contains circular imports or a nested package.json, leaving two high-risk analysis paths (cycle detection, monorepo boundary detection) without e2e regression coverage

All fixture projects use TypeScript exclusively — no fixture exercises the analyzer against a JavaScript-only codebase, leaving JS extension handling, import resolution differences, and component detection in .jsx files untested at the e2e level

The remix-app fixture uses route-based component organization but contains no shared component library directory (e.g., components/ or ui/), leaving the component-catalog analyzer untested against library-style co-located component hierarchies that are common in real Remix apps

The small-ts-project fixture has a flat two-level structure (models/, services/) — no fixture exercises deep nesting (3+ levels) or barrel-export patterns (index.ts re-exporting from siblings), which are the most common sources of false edges in import graph analysis

No fixture project contains .js or .jsx files. The analyzer's handling of JavaScript-only codebases (no tsconfig, .js extensions, CommonJS require() patterns) is entirely untested at the e2e level, creating a blind spot for a large category of real-world projects.

No fixture exercises barrel-export patterns (index.ts files that re-export from sibling modules). Barrel exports are the most common source of phantom import edges in static analysis — they make A→B appear as A→barrel→B, potentially inflating import counts and distorting zone boundaries. Absence of this fixture means the zone detection algorithm's correctness under barrel exports is unverified.

No fixture project exercises re-analysis of a directory that already contains a .sourcevision/ directory. The incremental update path — where the analyzer runs against a project it has previously analyzed — is entirely untested at the e2e level. This is distinct from the circular-import gap already noted and represents a different class of regression risk (state mutation vs. graph shape).

All fixture projects have flat or two-level directory structures. No fixture models a project where a subdirectory contains its own package.json (nested package scenario), leaving the inventory phase boundary detection untested against the most common real-world monorepo layout.

No fixture exercises the re-analysis (incremental update) path where .sourcevision/ already exists and the analyzer must decide what to preserve vs. recompute. This is a high-risk untested path: stale cache reads, partial overwrites, and schema version mismatches are all possible failure modes that would only surface during a second analysis run.

Fixture naming uses directory paths (remix-app, small-ts-project) rather than the capability being exercised (route-component-discovery, module-graph-analysis). When a new fixture is needed, there is no naming convention to follow, and the purpose of each existing fixture is discoverable only by reading its file contents.
[call graph] 5 internal calls, 0 outgoing, 0 incoming (cohesion: 1, coupling: 0)

zones.name: Workspace Root
zones.description: Root-level project documentation, workspace package manifest, TypeScript build config, and shared Vitest configuration.
insights: Isolated files — no import edges between 9 files, cohesion is unmeasurable (reported as 0)
Houses cross-cutting concern files (tsconfig, vitest.config.ts, package.json) that anchor the entire monorepo build
Documentation assets (ARCHITECTURE.md, WORKSPACE_DESIGN.md, README.md) and image files co-locate with build config, which is normal for a monorepo root
Zero imports in or out reflect the correct role of this zone as an inert configuration root
Zero cohesion and coupling are expected; root-level config and documentation files form a natural singleton zone with no import relationships.

tsconfig.tsbuildinfo is a TypeScript incremental build artifact that is regenerated on every build — its presence in workspace-root suggests build outputs may not be fully gitignored, which can cause spurious diffs in version control and bloat repository size over time

tsconfig.tsbuildinfo is a generated build artifact present in workspace-root — verify it is listed in .gitignore; if it is committed, remove it from source control and add the pattern to .gitignore to prevent build noise in diffs
Bidirectional coupling: "sourcevision-analysis-core" ↔ "static-analysis-services" (12+5 crossings) — consider extracting shared interface
Bidirectional coupling: "branch-risk-domain" ↔ "sourcevision-analysis-core" (8+8 crossings) — consider extracting shared interface
Bidirectional coupling: "data-file-schema" ↔ "sourcevision-analysis-core" (1+7 crossings) — consider extracting shared interface
Bidirectional coupling: "branch-collection-pipeline" ↔ "sourcevision-analysis-core" (4+2 crossings) — consider extracting shared interface
Bidirectional coupling: "branch-risk-domain" ↔ "data-file-schema" (1+1 crossings) — consider extracting shared interface
Size imbalance: largest zone has 87 files vs smallest with 2 — uneven decomposition

The unit-2 zone (87 files, cohesion 0.9) acts as the gravitational center of this codebase — it receives imports from analyzers, unit, unit-analyzers, and unit-cli while also exporting back to unit and unit-analyzers, creating a high-traffic hub that warrants monitoring for accretion of unrelated responsibilities

The bidirectional import relationship between unit and unit-2 (8 imports each direction) is the highest-risk structural pattern in this batch; introducing a shared-types or domain-primitives zone to hold the mutually imported contracts would resolve the cycle

The five zones in this batch divide cleanly into three tiers — config/docs (claude-workspace-config, workspace-root), infrastructure (static-analysis-services), and domain (branch-risk-domain, branch-collection-pipeline) — which reflects a healthy layered architecture; enforcing import direction (infrastructure → domain, never domain → infrastructure) would solidify this

The five algorithmically distinct zones (unit, unit-2, unit-cli, analyzers, unit-analyzers) collectively represent what is architecturally a single package, suggesting Louvain community detection fragmented a high-density import graph into too many partitions — a merge pass consolidating unit-2, unit, and analyzers would better reflect the actual architecture.

Bidirectional zone-level imports between unit-2↔unit (8 each way) and unit-2↔analyzers (12/5) indicate the package lacks a clear internal layering contract — establishing an explicit internal dependency order (schema → analyzers → CLI) and enforcing it with a lint rule or test would eliminate these cycles.

The test-fixture-projects zone is the only zone with no cross-zone coupling at all, correctly modeling fixture data as a leaf node in the dependency graph; all other zones should aspire to similarly directional import flows.

The data-file-schema micro-zone (3 files, cohesion 1.0) is a healthy extraction of a shared constant module, but its small size means it risks being re-absorbed into the core during future refactors — pinning it explicitly in the zone manifest preserves its intentional isolation.

Three of five zones (static-analysis-services, branch-risk-domain, branch-collection-pipeline) show coupling above 0.50, which is expected in a domain-centric package but warrants a zone-dependency diagram review to confirm all high-traffic edges are intentional.

The unit-2 zone is referenced by every other non-config zone in this batch; if it continues to grow (currently 87 files at cohesion 0.9) it risks becoming a monolith — set a file-count ceiling and extract sub-zones proactively.

Zone fragmentation across 5+ partitions for a single package obscures rather than clarifies architecture — consider collapsing into 2-3 semantically meaningful zones (schema-foundation, analysis-pipeline, cli-export) to make the internal structure legible to both tooling and humans.

No zone in this codebase has a coupling score above 0.1, which is unusually low and suggests the import-graph community detection is partitioning within a tightly connected component rather than between genuinely separate modules — validate zone boundaries against actual architectural intent.

The test-fixture-projects zone correctly contains only test-support files with no production source, exemplifying the '-tests' suffix convention in reverse: fixture zones need no suffix because they are inherently non-production by their path prefix.

src/analyzers/ contains files from 4 zones (branch-collection-pipeline, branch-risk-domain, sourcevision-analysis-core, static-analysis-services) — consider grouping into subdirectories to clarify architectural boundaries
File "src/util/paths.ts" has 89% of import neighbors in src/analyzers/ but lives in src/util/ — consider relocating

File "tests/fixtures/small-ts-project/src/models/user.ts" has 100% of import neighbors in tests/fixtures/small-ts-project/src/services/ but lives in tests/fixtures/small-ts-project/src/models/ — consider relocating

unit-2 imports 7 times from unit-cli while unit-cli also imports back into unit-2 (1 import) and unit (1 import) — domain code (unit-2) depending on CLI code (unit-cli) is an architectural inversion; CLI layers should be leaves that consume domain, never providers to it

A three-zone import cycle exists: unit-2 → unit-cli → unit → unit-2 (and unit-2 ↔ unit directly at 8 each). This ring cycle is the highest-severity structural issue in the codebase and prevents any zone from being extracted or replaced independently

unit-analyzers is the closest to a clean directional consumer: it sends 4 imports to unit-2 and 3 to unit, receives only 2 back from unit-2 — this near-DAG relationship is the healthiest non-trivial coupling pattern in the codebase and should be used as the model for refactoring other zones

The two isolated zones (claude-workspace-config, workspace-root) and the near-clean unit-analyzers together suggest the codebase has a healthy outer shell but a tangled core — refactoring should proceed from the outside inward, stabilizing analyzers and branch-collection-pipeline before tackling the unit/unit-2 cycle

No zone imports from branch-collection-pipeline according to the cross-zone import table — it is a pure sink zone (leaf node). This is architecturally correct for an orchestration adapter layer and should be documented as intentional to prevent future back-imports from creeping in.

The entire cross-zone import graph in this package is a star topology centered on unit-2/sourcevision-analysis-core — every other zone either feeds into it or draws from it, with no peer-to-peer zone relationships at all; this is a degenerate graph structure that collapses zone boundaries into a hub-and-spoke model

unit-analyzers is the only zone with a clearly asymmetric coupling profile: 7 total outbound imports (4 to unit-2, 3 to unit) and only 2 inbound — it behaves as a pure consumer, which is architecturally healthy and distinguishes it from the bidirectional zones

The 'root' and '.claude' zones (cohesion: 0) represent unclassified files that the Louvain algorithm could not assign to a coherent community — zero cohesion is a sentinel value indicating these are catch-all residuals, not intentional zones, and their files should be audited and reassigned

No zone in this batch has a clean unidirectional import relationship with any other zone except test-fixture-projects (isolated) and data-file-schema (inbound only) — every active zone participates in at least one cycle, suggesting the package lacks an enforced internal layering contract

The cross-zone import counts reveal that the highest-traffic edges (unit-2↔analyzers at 12+5, unit↔unit-2 at 8+8) are also the bidirectional ones — traffic volume and cycle risk are correlated, which means the most-used interfaces are also the least architecturally stable

The unit-2 → unit-cli import direction (7 imports from domain to CLI) violates the standard CLI-at-leaf-node principle where CLI modules are consumers, not providers. This inversion suggests CLI utility code has leaked into unit-cli that rightfully belongs in a shared utility or schema zone.

A three-zone ring cycle (unit-2 → unit-cli → unit → unit-2) combined with a direct two-zone cycle (unit ↔ unit-2) means all three high-traffic zones are mutually reachable — no single zone can be modified, versioned, or extracted without risk of cascading effects across all three.

The codebase has two distinct structural tiers: a healthy isolated shell (config zones + branch-collection-pipeline as sink) and a tangled core (unit-2 / unit / unit-cli / analyzers in mutual cycles). Decomposition should target the core first, extracting shared types into a neutral foundation zone to break cycles before any other refactoring.

Star topology: all cross-zone traffic routes through sourcevision-analysis-core (unit-2) with no peer-to-peer edges between any other zones — the graph has degenerated into a hub-and-spoke model where zone boundaries provide no actual isolation

Every zone with active cross-zone imports participates in at least one bidirectional cycle; the only zones without cycles are test-fixture-projects (isolated) and data-file-schema (inbound-only) — enforcing a one-way import policy via a failing test would surface all violations immediately

unit-analyzers is the sole consumer-only zone (7 outbound, 2 inbound) — its asymmetric profile makes it the best candidate to serve as a reference implementation for how other zones should relate to the core

branch-collection-pipeline is the only non-config zone with zero inbound cross-zone imports, correctly modeling it as a pipeline terminus. Preserving this sink property requires a lint rule or test assertion preventing other zones from importing it.

The unit-2 → unit-analyzers back-edge (2 imports) means the gravitational hub zone imports from a zone positioned as its downstream consumer — this specific inversion has not been explicitly identified; it means unit-analyzers cannot be safely refactored without auditing unit-2's dependency on it

No zone in this package defines cross-zone contracts through TypeScript interface files or barrel re-exports of type-only surfaces — all cross-zone coupling appears to use direct concrete module imports, making it structurally impossible to swap implementations or introduce mocks without modifying every import site

The absence of any dedicated schema-foundation or types-only zone forces all zones (unit-2, branch-risk-domain, static-analysis-services) to import shared data contracts through zones that also carry runtime implementation code — the missing abstraction layer is a schema zone with zero outbound runtime dependencies that all others can safely import without cycle risk

The 'unit' zone (17 files, cohesion 0.48) occupies an architecturally ambiguous mid-layer position: it imports from unit-2 (8 imports) and also receives 2 inbound imports (1 from unit-cli, 1 from analyzers). A zone with cohesion below 0.5 that participates in both inbound and outbound cross-zone traffic has no clear single responsibility — it is neither a clean consumer nor a clean producer, which makes it the most likely zone to accumulate unrelated files over time.

The cross-zone import graph contains 12 directed edges across 7 zones — an average out-degree of 1.7 edges per zone. For a single package, this density is high and indicates the Louvain partition created zones with overlapping functional concerns rather than distinct boundaries. A correctly partitioned package should have sparse inter-zone edges (average out-degree < 1.0) with direction flowing one way.

Three zones have cohesion at or near zero (root: 0, .claude: 0, analyzers: 0.21). Cohesion below 0.25 is a sentinel indicating the zone was formed by path proximity or algorithm residue rather than actual import clustering. These three zones together represent 19 files that the detection algorithm could not confidently assign — auditing and manually pinning them to semantically appropriate zones would improve the accuracy of all cohesion and coupling metrics across the package.

The coordination cost of the three-zone ring cycle (unit-2 → unit-cli → unit → unit-2) scales with team size: any interface change in one zone requires simultaneous review of all three zones to avoid breakage. At current scale this is manageable, but as the package grows, this cycle will become a bottleneck for parallel development — the cycle must be broken before the team expands or before the interface surface area grows significantly.

The import density between unit-2 and other zones (12+8+7+5+2 = 34 total cross-zone imports involving unit-2) versus the total cross-zone import count (45) means unit-2 accounts for 76% of all cross-zone traffic. A zone responsible for 76% of cross-package communication is not a zone — it is the package with satellites, and the zone model has zero explanatory power at this ratio.

unit-2 imports back into unit-analyzers (2 imports) despite unit-analyzers being positioned as a downstream consumer of unit-2 — this reversal creates a hidden cycle between the hub zone and its presumed-downstream zone, undermining the near-clean asymmetric profile that made unit-analyzers the healthiest coupling pattern in the codebase

No interface or contract boundary exists between any two zones — all cross-zone imports couple directly to concrete module implementations rather than to declared interface types; introducing a single interface-only barrel per zone boundary would allow the compiler to detect abstraction violations and enable independent zone replacement

schema/v1.ts (canonical data contract) is reachable only through branch-risk-domain, which is enmeshed in bidirectional cycles — any zone that needs only the schema type must transitively import the entire domain cycle; a zero-dependency schema-foundation zone is the missing abstraction layer that would resolve this for all consumers simultaneously

Zone detection has produced a degenerate partition: one zone (sourcevision-analysis-core) holds 60% of files and 76% of cross-zone import traffic, while three other zones have cohesion below 0.25. The partition provides no actionable architectural information — it must be recomputed with a higher resolution target (target 8–12 zones of 10–20 files each) or supplemented with manually pinned zone boundaries before any zone-based architectural enforcement can be meaningful.

No zone in the codebase enforces its boundaries with a test or lint rule. The three-zone ring cycle (unit-2/unit/unit-cli), the domain→CLI inversion (unit-2 importing from unit-cli), and the bidirectional unit↔unit-2 coupling are all detectable as static assertions but none are currently asserted. Without enforcement, architectural drift will re-introduce cycles after any refactor that eliminates them.

The 'analyzers' zone (8 files, cohesion 0.21) and 'unit-analyzers' zone (5 files, cohesion 0.20) both have sub-0.25 cohesion and both import heavily from sourcevision-analysis-core. Two low-cohesion satellite zones with similar names and overlapping import targets strongly suggest they should be merged into a single zone or absorbed into the core — their separate existence adds labeling complexity without providing architectural clarity.

The unit/unit-2/unit-cli/unit-analyzers naming family has no filesystem anchor — none of these zone names correspond to a source directory named 'unit', making the names Louvain algorithm labels with no navigable path equivalent; renaming zones to mirror their dominant source directory (e.g. src/analyzers → analysis-core, src/cli → cli-commands) would make zone boundaries self-documenting and navigable without tooling

No zone in this codebase uses a TypeScript barrel file (index.ts) as an explicit public API boundary — all cross-zone imports reference concrete implementation file paths directly; without barrel boundaries the TypeScript compiler cannot enforce zone-level API surfaces, and any internal file restructuring silently breaks external consumers

The two zones with the worst cohesion/coupling profiles (static-analysis-services 0.21/0.79, branch-collection-pipeline 0.20/0.80) share a structural signature — minimal internal coherence paired with maximal external coupling — that is the fingerprint of a pure glue/adapter layer that has no stable identity of its own; these zones are better candidates for dissolution into their highest-coupling neighbors than for internal refactoring

The Louvain partition assigns files to zones based on import-graph community structure, but four of the seven active zones have cohesion below 0.50, indicating the algorithm fragmented a dense import graph into partitions that do not correspond to genuine semantic boundaries; supplementing algorithmic detection with manually pinned zone anchors for the three highest-traffic files (public.ts, constants.ts, schema/v1.ts) would improve metric accuracy across all zones simultaneously

Bimodal cohesion distribution: zones are either high-cohesion (≥0.9) or low-cohesion (≤0.48). No zone has cohesion in the 0.5–0.85 range. This bimodal gap indicates the Louvain algorithm created one dominant cluster and several artifact satellites rather than a balanced partition — the missing middle tier is where functional domain boundaries should live.

The highest-traffic bidirectional edge (unit-2↔analyzers: 12+5=17 total) has a 2.4:1 asymmetry ratio — the highest of any bidirectional pair. High traffic + high asymmetry is the signature of an artificially split zone: 'analyzers' is functionally an extension of unit-2 that Louvain separated due to path-level clustering rather than import clustering. Merging these two zones would reduce total bidirectional edge count from 3 to 2.

The three zones with cohesion below 0.25 (root: 0, .claude: 0, analyzers: 0.21) represent files the Louvain algorithm could not confidently assign. Filtering or dissolving sub-0.25-cohesion zones before reporting would produce more actionable architectural statistics — their presence inflates the zone count and makes average cohesion metrics less meaningful.

The package has zero production zones that act as pure sinks (inbound-only). Every active production zone participates in at least one outbound cross-zone edge. In a well-layered package, leaf/orchestration zones should be pure sinks. The absence of any pure-sink production zone confirms there is no enforced layering contract in the non-foundation portion of the package.

Rename all unit-prefixed zones (unit, unit-2, unit-cli, unit-analyzers) to names that reflect their dominant source directory or architectural role — algorithm-generated labels with no filesystem anchor make zone-to-directory mapping impossible without tooling and obscure dependency direction

Introduce a TypeScript barrel file (index.ts or public-api.ts) per zone that re-exports only the zone's intended public surface — currently all cross-zone imports reference concrete implementation paths, preventing compiler enforcement of zone contracts and making internal restructuring silently breaking

Evaluate dissolving both static-analysis-services (cohesion 0.21, coupling 0.79) and branch-collection-pipeline (cohesion 0.20, coupling 0.80) rather than refactoring them — both exhibit the glue-layer structural signature (near-zero cohesion, near-maximum coupling) and their files would be better assigned directly to the zones they most heavily couple with, eliminating two low-signal zone labels from the architecture map

The package has no enforced internal layering contract. The desired order (schema → analyzers → CLI) is never asserted as a test or lint rule. All three known cycles (unit↔unit-2 bidirectional, unit-2→unit-cli domain-to-leaf inversion, three-zone ring cycle) are detectable as static import assertions but none are currently checked. A single test file asserting forbidden import directions would surface all three violations immediately and prevent regression after any refactoring.

Zone naming is inconsistent: three zones use semantic names (sourcevision-analysis-core, data-file-schema, test-fixture-projects) while five zones use algorithm-generated labels (unit, unit-2, unit-cli, unit-analyzers, analyzers). Renaming algorithm-generated zones to semantic names as a single batch operation would make the architecture legible without requiring any code changes.

The only two zones with zero coupling (data-file-schema via functional design, test-fixture-projects via path isolation) achieve isolation through different mechanisms. The package has no third example of an intentionally isolated zone. Documenting these two isolation patterns as canonical reference models and identifying which other zones could adopt each pattern would give contributors a concrete target for the next refactoring cycle.

findings.text: High coupling (0.8) — 4 imports target "sourcevision-analysis-core"
Low cohesion (0.2) — files are loosely related, consider splitting this zone

Cohesion 0.20 and coupling 0.80 are at warning thresholds; as an orchestration-layer adapter this is acceptable if the zone deliberately wires collector → CLI, but document this intent to prevent accidental logic accumulation.

The CLI command (pr-markdown.ts) and its domain service (branch-work-collector.ts) are correctly co-located here, keeping the CLI thin and the domain logic in unit; this is a positive structural pattern.
High coupling (0.52) — 8 imports target "sourcevision-analysis-core"
Bidirectional coupling with unit-2 (8 imports in each direction) creates a circular dependency smell at the zone level; decompose shared types into a neutral foundation zone to break the cycle.

Hosting both the public API entrypoint (public.ts) and implementation details (branch-work-store, risk-scoring) in the same zone makes the public surface hard to audit; consider pinning entrypoint and schema files to a dedicated api-surface zone.
Isolated files — no import edges between 2 files, cohesion is unmeasurable (reported as 0)
Zero cohesion and zero coupling are expected here; this zone is configuration-only and should not be evaluated against code quality thresholds.
High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
Perfect cohesion and zero coupling make this an ideal candidate to pin as a foundation-level constant module, explicitly positioned below the analysis core in the dependency hierarchy.
Seven inbound imports from the analysis core confirm data-files.ts is a shared constant dependency; keeping it isolated here prevents the constants from being entangled with analyzer logic.

The mcp.test.ts file tests CLI MCP behavior but lives in this schema zone due to import-graph proximity — verify it belongs here architecturally or consider moving it closer to the CLI entry point tests.
Bidirectional coupling: "sourcevision-analysis-core" ↔ "static-analysis-services" (12+5 crossings) — consider extracting shared interface
Size imbalance: largest zone has 87 files vs smallest with 2 — uneven decomposition
Fan-in hotspot: src/cli/output.ts receives calls from 13 files (high fan-in expected for this module type) — high-impact module, changes may have wide ripple effects
11 entry points — wide API surface, consider consolidating exports
Contains 60% of project files (87/146) — subdivided into 7 sub-zones

Bidirectional imports with both 'Static Analysis Services' (12 out / 5 in) and 'Branch Risk Domain' (8 each way) create zone-level cycles that obscure actual dependency direction — the shared dependencies should be pushed into a common foundation module.

Entry points span schema, CLI, and analyzer layers simultaneously, which is appropriate for a package core but means consumers must navigate multiple entry surfaces — the public.ts barrel should remain the canonical single entry point.
Zone has exemplary internal cohesion (0.9) and very low external coupling (0.1), indicating a well-bounded implementation layer.
High coupling (0.79) — 5 imports target "sourcevision-analysis-core"
Low cohesion (0.21) — files are loosely related, consider splitting this zone

Cohesion 0.21 and coupling 0.79 exceed warning thresholds; the zone is pulled in by 12 imports from unit-2 alone, suggesting it may be serving as a dumping ground for shared analysis primitives rather than a focused module.
callgraph-findings.ts appears to have no corresponding test file while its sibling services (archetypes, completion-reader, manifest) are all tested — close this coverage gap.
High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
Fixtures are fully self-contained with no production imports, which is the correct pattern — any drift toward importing from src/ would indicate test infrastructure coupling and should be reverted.
The Remix fixture provides a realistic multi-file route hierarchy for testing React component and import-graph analyzers against non-trivial real-world structure.
There is no fixture for a project with circular dependencies or monorepo structure — adding one would improve confidence in the zone-detection and cycle-detection paths.
Isolated files — no import edges between 9 files, cohesion is unmeasurable (reported as 0)
Zero cohesion and coupling are expected; root-level config and documentation files form a natural singleton zone with no import relationships.

5 files across 5 distinct directories with zero directory co-location — the zone is held together only by import proximity; evaluate dissolving it entirely by pinning branch-work-collector.ts to the domain zone it couples with and pr-markdown.ts to a cli-commands zone, rather than treating the glue layer as an architectural unit
Zone "Branch Collection Pipeline" (branch-collection-pipeline) has catastrophic risk (score: 0.80, cohesion: 0.20, coupling: 0.80) — requires immediate architectural intervention
Zone "branch-risk-domain" has files across 7 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory

public.ts entrypoint zone assignment is algorithm-determined and not stable across re-analysis runs — pin public.ts to a manually-curated zone in the zone manifest to prevent silent entrypoint drift after future graph topology changes

src/analyzers/ contains files from 4 zones (branch-collection-pipeline, branch-risk-domain, sourcevision-analysis-core, static-analysis-services) — consider grouping into subdirectories to clarify architectural boundaries

Evaluate dissolving both static-analysis-services (cohesion 0.21, coupling 0.79) and branch-collection-pipeline (cohesion 0.20, coupling 0.80) rather than refactoring them — both exhibit the glue-layer structural signature (near-zero cohesion, near-maximum coupling) and their files would be better assigned directly to the zones they most heavily couple with, eliminating two low-signal zone labels from the architecture map

Introduce a TypeScript barrel file (index.ts or public-api.ts) per zone that re-exports only the zone's intended public surface — currently all cross-zone imports reference concrete implementation paths, preventing compiler enforcement of zone contracts and making internal restructuring silently breaking

Rename all unit-prefixed zones (unit, unit-2, unit-cli, unit-analyzers) to names that reflect their dominant source directory or architectural role — algorithm-generated labels with no filesystem anchor make zone-to-directory mapping impossible without tooling and obscure dependency direction

The only two zones with zero coupling (data-file-schema via functional design, test-fixture-projects via path isolation) achieve isolation through different mechanisms. The package has no third example of an intentionally isolated zone. Documenting these two isolation patterns as canonical reference models and identifying which other zones could adopt each pattern would give contributors a concrete target for the next refactoring cycle.

The package has no enforced internal layering contract. The desired order (schema → analyzers → CLI) is never asserted as a test or lint rule. All three known cycles (unit↔unit-2 bidirectional, unit-2→unit-cli domain-to-leaf inversion, three-zone ring cycle) are detectable as static import assertions but none are currently checked. A single test file asserting forbidden import directions would surface all three violations immediately and prevent regression after any refactoring.

Zone naming is inconsistent: three zones use semantic names (sourcevision-analysis-core, data-file-schema, test-fixture-projects) while five zones use algorithm-generated labels (unit, unit-2, unit-cli, unit-analyzers, analyzers). Renaming algorithm-generated zones to semantic names as a single batch operation would make the architecture legible without requiring any code changes.
Hub function: info in src/cli/output.ts is called from 9 files (high fan-in expected for this module type) — changes here have wide impact, consider if responsibilities can be narrowed
2 zones exceed architectural risk thresholds (cohesion < 0.4, coupling > 0.6): branch-collection-pipeline, static-analysis-services — mandatory refactoring recommended before further development
Zone "sourcevision-analysis-core" has files across 12 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory

No index.ts or public barrel file exists at the zone boundary to make the external API surface explicit. All 87 files are directly importable by other zones, meaning zone membership is enforced only by community-detection metadata, not by code structure. Any new import added to any of the 87 files silently extends the zone boundary.

Zone imports from unit-cli (7 imports: domain consuming leaf/CLI layer) while also correctly importing from data-file-schema (foundation). This barbell profile — consuming both the foundation layer and the leaf layer simultaneously — means the zone has no stable tier position and cannot be isolated for independent versioning or replacement.
Zone "test-fixture-projects" has files across 5 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory

Fixture naming uses directory paths (remix-app, small-ts-project) rather than the capability being exercised (route-component-discovery, module-graph-analysis). When a new fixture is needed, there is no naming convention to follow, and the purpose of each existing fixture is discoverable only by reading its file contents.

No fixture exercises the re-analysis (incremental update) path where .sourcevision/ already exists and the analyzer must decide what to preserve vs. recompute. This is a high-risk untested path: stale cache reads, partial overwrites, and schema version mismatches are all possible failure modes that would only surface during a second analysis run.
File "src/util/paths.ts" has 89% of import neighbors in src/analyzers/ but lives in src/util/ — consider relocating

File "tests/fixtures/small-ts-project/src/models/user.ts" has 100% of import neighbors in tests/fixtures/small-ts-project/src/services/ but lives in tests/fixtures/small-ts-project/src/models/ — consider relocating

public.ts residing in a zone that participates in an 8+8 bidirectional cycle with unit-2 means the package's public API is coupled to internal implementation cycles — pin public.ts and schema/v1.ts to an isolated api-surface zone with only downward imports to stabilize the contract boundary.

data-file-schema is the only non-fixture zone with zero outbound cross-zone imports — it is the sole genuine foundation layer in this codebase and should be explicitly protected from gaining any outbound edges

sourcevision-analysis-core is the sole mandatory intermediary for all cross-zone traffic in the package — zero zones communicate with each other without passing through it, making it a single point of architectural failure for any zone-boundary refactor

Fixture coverage gap: no fixture project contains circular imports or a nested package.json, leaving two high-risk analysis paths (cycle detection, monorepo boundary detection) without e2e regression coverage

test-fixture-projects is the only zone with confirmed zero cross-zone coupling in both directions — it is the reference model for what a correctly bounded zone looks like and its pattern (path-prefixed isolation, no src/ imports) should be codified as a lint rule

branch-collection-pipeline connects to the rest of the codebase exclusively via unit-2 as a single coupling point — this makes unit-2 a mandatory transitive dependency for any consumer of the collection pipeline, increasing blast radius if unit-2 is refactored.

claude-workspace-config and workspace-root together form a cleanly isolated configuration stratum with zero cross-zone imports in either direction — this boundary is the healthiest in the codebase and should be explicitly preserved.
mcp.test.ts lives in data-file-schema but tests CLI MCP behavior — its zone placement is an artifact of import-graph proximity, not architectural intent, and will silently diverge as CLI code evolves

unit-cli participates in a three-node cycle: unit-2 → unit-cli (7 imports) and unit-cli → unit-2 (1 import) closes a direct cycle, while unit-cli → unit (1 import) and unit → unit-2 (8 imports) closes a second indirect cycle through unit — two overlapping cycles sharing one edge

The 12-inbound / 5-outbound import asymmetry with unit-2 indicates analyzers acts as a shared utility layer that has grown a back-dependency on its primary consumer — extract the shared contracts into a neutral schema zone to make the relationship strictly one-directional.

CLI command (pr-markdown.ts) and domain service (branch-work-collector.ts) are in the same zone — split along the CLI/domain boundary so the domain service can be imported independently of CLI infrastructure

tests/integration/pr-markdown-reviewer-output.test.ts validates markdown rendering output but is assigned to the collection pipeline zone — misassigned test obscures which production code it guards and should be co-located with the template code in branch-risk-domain

branch-work-store.ts co-locates stateful persistence with stateless domain logic (classifier, filter, risk-scorer) — the store abstraction belongs in a separate persistence or state zone to preserve the functional purity of the domain layer

pr-markdown-template.ts (presentation/rendering) is embedded in the domain zone alongside classifiers and risk-scorers — presentation templates should reside in a dedicated output or generators zone, not in the domain layer

schema/v1.ts (the package's canonical data contract) lives inside a zone that participates in an 8+8 bidirectional cycle with unit-2 — the schema is transitively entangled in implementation cycles, making it impossible to depend on the contract without pulling in all of branch-risk-domain's coupling baggage; extract schema/v1.ts to an isolated schema-foundation zone with zero outbound runtime dependencies

mcp.test.ts is discoverable only by import-graph proximity, not by file path or zone membership. Developers debugging CLI MCP behavior will search the CLI zone for tests and miss this file entirely, leading to duplicate test authoring or untested regression paths during CLI refactors.

The 'analyzers' sub-zone (8 files, cohesion 0.21) has the lowest cohesion of any named zone, indicating its 8 files do not form a natural cluster. Files grouped there likely share a directory path rather than import relationships — cohesion below 0.3 in an 8-file zone suggests artificial grouping that obscures actual dependencies.

Zone encompasses 87 files at cohesion 0.9, effectively absorbing the entire package into a single unit. Zone detection has failed to partition the codebase into actionable boundaries — the 7 internal sub-zones should be elevated to top-level zones so that cross-zone import analysis reflects real architectural intent rather than a single monolithic blob vs. a few satellite micro-zones.

completion-reader.ts introduces file-system I/O into a zone labeled as analysis services — I/O adapters and pure analysis logic are different abstraction levels and should reside in separate zones to allow hermetic testing

constants.ts defines shared type contracts but is co-located with service implementations, forcing consumers to couple to the full service zone to access shared types — extract to a neutral schema or types zone with zero runtime dependencies

No fixture exercises barrel-export patterns (index.ts files that re-export from sibling modules). Barrel exports are the most common source of phantom import edges in static analysis — they make A→B appear as A→barrel→B, potentially inflating import counts and distorting zone boundaries. Absence of this fixture means the zone detection algorithm's correctness under barrel exports is unverified.

No fixture project contains .js or .jsx files. The analyzer's handling of JavaScript-only codebases (no tsconfig, .js extensions, CommonJS require() patterns) is entirely untested at the e2e level, creating a blind spot for a large category of real-world projects.

Cohesion 0.21 + 17 cross-zone import edges = highest fragility score in the package. Files in this zone have no strong import-based reason to co-exist, yet are heavily coupled to the package core. A change to any file here cannot be safely scoped. Auditing the 8 files to assign each to its primary importing zone and dissolving this zone would reduce the number of bidirectional cycles from 3 to 2.

Zone name 'analyzers' collides with directory name src/analyzers/ but the zone does not contain all files in that directory. Files in src/analyzers/ belong to at least 4 different zones with no visible marker per file. This naming collision will cause developer confusion when navigating by directory vs. by zone.

No automated assertion prevents data-files.ts from gaining outbound imports. A single accidental import in this file would destroy the only zero-coupling foundation zone in the package. Adding a test that asserts data-files.ts has no non-type imports would protect the invariant that makes this zone valuable.

Zone cohesion 1.0 is an artifact of test file co-location with production source, not genuine functional cohesion. This creates an inconsistency: every other zone stores tests under tests/, but data-file-schema stores them alongside src/. Documenting this as an intentional pattern (or relocating the tests) prevents future maintainers from normalizing the co-location and accidentally dissolving the zone boundary.

Three distinct abstraction layers (type registry via constants.ts, I/O adapter via completion-reader.ts, stateless transformers via archetypes/manifest/callgraph-findings) coexist in one zone and mechanically produce cohesion 0.21 — dissolve the zone by assigning each abstraction tier to its appropriate target zone rather than attempting an internal refactor

src/constants.ts sits in src/ root while all peer files live in src/analyzers/ — relocate to src/analyzers/constants.ts or a dedicated src/schema/ directory to make zone membership visible through directory structure alone without requiring tooling
Zone "Static Analysis Services" (static-analysis-services) has catastrophic risk (score: 0.79, cohesion: 0.21, coupling: 0.79) — requires immediate architectural intervention

Zone participates in two simultaneous import cycles: a direct two-zone cycle with unit-2 (8 imports each way) and a three-zone ring cycle through unit-cli. No file in this zone can be safely modified, relocated, or deleted without auditing both cycles. Before any refactoring of this zone, a static dependency graph of the 17 files must be produced to identify which files are on each cycle path and which (if any) are cycle-free.

The 2 inbound imports from unit-2 into unit-analyzers mean the hub zone depends on its downstream consumer. This specific reverse edge would block extracting unit-analyzers into a separate package. Identifying and eliminating these 2 imports (or relocating the imported symbols into unit-2) is a prerequisite for any future modularization effort.

Zone name 'unit-analyzers' is ambiguous — it reads as either 'unit tests for the analyzers zone' or 'the analyzer sub-zone of the unit zone'. Since this is a production code zone, a name encoding its functional role would eliminate the test/production ambiguity and make the zone manifest self-documenting.

tsconfig.tsbuildinfo is a generated build artifact present in workspace-root — verify it is listed in .gitignore; if it is committed, remove it from source control and add the pattern to .gitignore to prevent build noise in diffs
Zone "Workspace Root" (workspace-root) has at-risk risk (score: 0.50, cohesion: 0.00, coupling: 0.00) — approaching architectural risk thresholds
God function: cmdAnalyze in src/cli/commands/analyze.ts calls 32 unique functions — consider decomposing into smaller, focused functions
Zone "Claude Workspace Config" (claude-workspace-config) has at-risk risk (score: 0.50, cohesion: 0.00, coupling: 0.00) — unreliable: zone has only 2 files (minimum 5 for reliable metrics)
